lessons.title: Nitrogen, Ammonia and the Haber Process

content.body: Nitrogen makes up 78% of the atmosphere but is unreactive (very stable N≡N triple bond). It must be 'fixed' (combined with other elements) to be useful.
AMMONIA (NH₃): A colourless gas with a pungent smell. It is alkaline, dissolves readily in water to form ammonium hydroxide.

HABER PROCESS — Industrial manufacture of ammonia:
N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g) ΔH = −92 kJ/mol
Conditions: Temperature 450°C, Pressure 200 atmospheres, Iron catalyst
USES OF AMMONIA: Making fertilisers (ammonium sulphate, ammonium nitrate, urea), making nitric acid, making explosives, as a refrigerant.

Making ammonium nitrate fertiliser:
NH₃ + HNO₃ → NH₄NO₃
(ammonia + nitric acid → ammonium nitrate)

content.title: Why these conditions?
content.body:
• 450°C: compromise — higher temp gives more yield but reaction too slow at low temp; too high temp shifts equilibrium backwards (exothermic)
• 200 atm: high pressure favours fewer moles of gas (4 moles → 2 moles), increasing yield
• Iron catalyst: speeds up reaction without changing yield

questions.question: What percentage of the atmosphere is nitrogen?
questions.explanation: Air is approximately 78% N₂, 21% O₂, 1% other gases.

questions.question: The Haber process makes:
questions.explanation: The Haber process: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃ (using iron catalyst, 450°C, 200 atm).

questions.question: The catalyst used in the Haber process is:
questions.explanation: Iron catalyst speeds up the equilibrium reaction without affecting the final yield.

questions.question: Why is high pressure used in the Haber process?
options: Left side has more moles of gas — high pressure favours NH₃ production
questions.answer: Left side has more moles of gas — high pressure favours NH₃ production
questions.explanation: 4 moles of gas → 2 moles. Le Chatelier: high pressure shifts equilibrium towards fewer moles = more NH₃.

questions.question: Ammonia solution is:
questions.explanation: NH₃ + H₂O ⇌ NH₄⁺ + OH⁻. Ammonium hydroxide releases OH⁻ ions → alkaline pH.

questions.question: The main use of ammonia is:
questions.explanation: ~80% of ammonia produced globally is used to make nitrogen fertilisers (ammonium nitrate, urea, etc).

questions.question: Ammonium nitrate is formed by:
questions.explanation: Ammonia + nitric acid → ammonium nitrate. NH₃ + HNO₃ → NH₄NO₃

questions.question: Why is 450°C used in the Haber process rather than a lower temperature?
options: Lower temperature is too slow even with catalyst
To keep ammonia as a gas
questions.answer: Lower temperature is too slow even with catalyst
questions.explanation: Although lower temperature gives higher equilibrium yield, rate is too slow. 450°C is a compromise.

questions.question: Which test confirms ammonia gas?
questions.explanation: NH₃ is the only common alkaline gas — it turns damp red litmus blue.

questions.question: The Haber process reaction is reversible because:
options: The reaction can go in both directions at the same time
questions.answer: The reaction can go in both directions at the same time
questions.explanation: The ⇌ symbol shows a reversible reaction reaching dynamic equilibrium.

questions.question: Nitrogen 'fixation' means:
options: Converting atmospheric N₂ into useful nitrogen compounds
questions.answer: Converting atmospheric N₂ into useful nitrogen compounds
questions.explanation: Nitrogen fixation converts unreactive N₂ gas into compounds plants and animals can use.

questions.question: Urea [(NH₂)₂CO] is an important fertiliser because:
questions.explanation: Urea contains 46% nitrogen by mass — the highest nitrogen content of any solid fertiliser.

questions.question: Nitrogen is unreactive because:
options: It has a very strong triple bond (N≡N)
questions.answer: It has a very strong triple bond (N≡N)
questions.explanation: The N≡N triple bond requires very high energy to break, making nitrogen extremely stable and unreactive.

questions.question: Hydrogen for the Haber process comes from:
questions.explanation: CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ (steam reforming of natural gas is the main source of H₂ for Haber process).

questions.question: Excess nitrogen fertiliser in soil can cause:
options: Soil to become more fertile indefinitely
questions.explanation: Nitrates leach into rivers/lakes, causing algal bloom → death of aquatic life (eutrophication).

questions.question: Ammonia + sulphuric acid → ?
questions.explanation: 2NH₃ + H₂SO₄ → (NH₄)₂SO₄ (ammonium sulphate) — a common fertiliser.

questions.question: The Ostwald process converts ammonia to:
questions.explanation: Ostwald process: NH₃ + O₂ (Pt catalyst, 850°C) → NO → NO₂ → HNO₃ (nitric acid).

questions.question: In the Haber process, unreacted gases are:
questions.explanation: Unreacted N₂ and H₂ are recycled to increase overall yield and reduce waste.

questions.question: Nitrogen gas (N₂) is used in food packaging because:
options: It is unreactive and prevents oxidation/spoilage
questions.answer: It is unreactive and prevents oxidation/spoilage
questions.explanation: N₂ displaces O₂ in food packaging, preventing oxidation and bacterial growth.

questions.question: The equation N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ shows that to make 2 moles of NH₃, you need:
options: 1 mole each of N₂ and H₂
questions.explanation: Molar ratio from equation: 1:3:2. One mole N₂ reacts with three moles H₂.
